Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer uses SPECIFIC NEGATIVE DEMEANOR WORDS describing the representative's manner, tone, or interpersonal behavior (including but not limited to: rude, nasty, disrespectful, condescending, arrogant, dismissive, hostile, unprofessional, cold, impersonal, robotic, or similar negative demeanor descriptors). Empathy-absence phrasing also qualifies (e.g., "no empathy," "treated me like a number," "felt like I was bothering them"). Unprofessional conduct phrasing also qualifies (e.g., "behavior was unacceptable," "acted out of line"). GENERIC NEGATIVE WORDS ALONE (e.g., "terrible," "awful," "horrible," "bad") do NOT qualify unless accompanied by a demeanor descriptor. EXCLUDE: Background noise/technical issues (not rep demeanor). Perceived Trust (lied, misled) ONLY qualifies if explicitly attributed to the CURRENT rep, not previous reps or the company generally. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: grosero, maleducado, irrespetuoso, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: New sub-topic. Definition sourced from revised classification prompt.
Taxonomy: Rep Not Courteous->Customer uses SPECIFIC NEGATIVE DEMEANOR WORDS describing the representative's manner, tone, or interpersonal behavior (including but not limited to: rude, dismissive, disrespectful, condescending, impatient, hostile, pushy, unprofessional, or similar negative demeanor descriptors). GENERIC NEGATIVE WORDS ALONE (e.g., "terrible," "awful," "horrible," "bad") do NOT qualify unless accompanied by a demeanor descriptor. EXCLUDE: Background noise/technical issues (not rep demeanor). Perceived Trust (lied, misled) ONLY qualifies if explicitly attributed to the CURRENT rep, not previous reps or the company generally. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: grosero, maleducado, irrespetuoso, or similar Spanish negative demeanor words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's interpersonal conduct and demeanor during the interaction — encompassing tone, warmth, courtesy, respect, empathy, emotional ownership, patience, and professional standards.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer uses SPECIFIC DEMEANOR WORDS describing the representative's manner, tone, or interpersonal behavior (including but not limited to: polite, friendly, kind, respectful, professional, patient, courteous, understanding, empathetic, warm, attentive, accommodating, composed, business-like, or similar demeanor descriptors). Patience-specific phrasing also qualifies (e.g., "took time to explain," "never rushed me"). GENERIC PRAISE ALONE (e.g., "great," "fantastic," "excellent," "amazing," "awesome," "the best") does NOT qualify unless accompanied by a demeanor descriptor. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: amable, educado, paciente, muy profesional, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: New sub-topic. Definition sourced from revised classification prompt.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Courteous->Customer uses SPECIFIC DEMEANOR WORDS describing the representative's manner, tone, or interpersonal behavior (including but not limited to: polite, friendly, nice, kind, respectful, professional, patient, pleasant, sweet, courteous, understanding, empathetic, personable, gracious, warm, caring, considerate, attentive, accommodating, or similar demeanor descriptors). GENERIC PRAISE ALONE (e.g., "great," "fantastic," "excellent," "amazing," "awesome," "the best") does NOT qualify unless accompanied by a demeanor descriptor. This theme measures HOW the rep interacted, not WHAT they accomplished. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: amable, cortés, educado, paciente, atento, or similar Spanish demeanor words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's interpersonal conduct and demeanor during the interaction — encompassing tone, warmth, courtesy, respect, empathy, emotional ownership, patience, and professional standards.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Rep rushed the customer, was impatient, or made them feel hurried (including but not limited to: "too fast," "rushed me," "didn't take time," "felt hurried," "was not patient," "wanted to get me off the phone," "seemed like they were in a rush," "cut me short," "didn't let me finish," or similar phrasing). Does NOT apply to: (1) deliberate dismissive attitude or demeanor without a pacing/hurrying component (see Rep Not Courteous). CO-CLASSIFY: Rep Not Courteous.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Impatient / Rushed->Rep rushed the customer was impatient or made them feel hurried. Include: 'too fast' 'rushed me' 'didn't take time' 'felt hurried' 'was not patient' 'wanted to get me off the phone' 'seemed like they were in a rush' 'cut me short' 'didn't let me finish'. Exclude dismissive/rude (separate sub-topic).

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's interpersonal conduct and demeanor during the interaction — encompassing tone, warmth, courtesy, respect, empathy, emotional ownership, patience, and professional standards.
Sub_Topic: Rep Was Pushy / Aggressive Sales Approach
Sub_Topic_Definition: Rep was overly aggressive in selling or pushing products and services the customer did not want (including but not limited to: "too pushy," "kept trying to sell me," "wouldn't take no," "hard sell," "wouldn't stop pushing," "forced products on me," "pressured me to add services," or similar sales pressure language). Does NOT apply to: (1) rep recommending or offering products without explicit customer pushback (helpful upsell, not applicable here). CO-CLASSIFY: Rep Not Courteous.
Notes: Very low volume signal. Consider aggregating with nearest sibling unless a distinct operational owner requires separate tracking.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Pushy / Aggressive Sales Approach->Rep was overly aggressive in selling or pushing products and services the customer did not want. Include: 'too pushy' 'kept trying to sell me' 'wouldn't take no' 'aggressive' 'hard sell' 'wouldn't stop pushing' 'forced products on me' 'pressured me to add services'. Distinct from helpful upsell — customer explicitly felt pressure.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic: Rep Made the Process Easy / Handled Everything
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer describes the interaction as effortless or the rep handling everything without burdening them (including but not limited to: "made it so easy", "seamless", "handled everything for me", "I didn't have to do anything", "handled it completely", or similar language). Customer Effort Score (CES) signal — distinct from Rep Was Helpful (outcome achieved) by focusing on ease of experience rather than resolution alone.
Notes: New sub-topic. CES alignment signal — tracks customer perception of effort reduction.
Taxonomy: Rep Made the Process Easy / Handled Everything->Customer describes the interaction as effortless or the rep handling everything without burdening them. Include: 'made it so easy' 'seamless' 'handled everything for me' 'I didn't have to do anything' 'took care of it all' 'no hassle' 'made it simple' 'didn't have to worry about a thing' 'handled it completely'. Customer Effort Score (CES) signal — distinct from Rep Was Helpful (outcome achieved) by focusing on ease of experience rather than resolution alone.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic: Rep Proactively Flagged an Issue / Heads-Up
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer notes the rep volunteered information or flagged something the customer had not noticed or asked about (including but not limited to: "told me about a discount I didn't know about", "spotted an issue I didn't notice", "warned me before it became a problem", "brought something to my attention", or similar language). Anticipatory service signal — distinct from Rep Was Helpful (reactive) by the proactive nature of the action.
Notes: New sub-topic. Proactive service behavior — high coaching value for identifying reps who anticipate customer needs.
Taxonomy: Rep Proactively Flagged an Issue / Heads-Up->Customer notes the rep volunteered information or flagged something the customer had not noticed or asked about. Include: 'told me about a discount I didn't know about' 'noticed something on my account' 'flagged a charge before I saw it' 'proactively mentioned' 'brought it to my attention' 'didn't wait for me to ask' 'spotted an issue I didn't catch' 'warned me before it became a problem'. Anticipatory service signal — distinct from Rep Was Helpful (reactive) by the proactive nature of the action.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer praises the speed or efficiency of the resolution without implying the rep rushed them (including but not limited to: "quick", "fast", "efficient", "handled it right away", "in and out", "no runaround", "resolved in minutes", "one call and done", "fixed it immediately", "straight to the point", or similar language). Distinct from Rep Was Patient (time given to customer) — this is about speed of resolution, not pace of interaction.
Notes: New sub-topic. Efficiency signal — complements Rep Was Patient (positive time experience) by capturing praise for fast resolution.
Taxonomy: Rep Resolved Issue Quickly / Efficiently->Customer praises the speed or efficiency of the resolution without implying the rep rushed them. Include: 'quick' 'fast' 'efficient' 'handled it right away' 'in and out' 'no runaround' 'resolved in minutes' 'one call and done' 'fixed it immediately' 'didn't waste my time' 'straight to the point'. Distinct from Rep Was Patient (time given to customer) — this is about speed of resolution, not pace of interaction.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Rep confirmed the issue was fully resolved and checked in with the customer before ending the interaction (including but not limited to: "made sure everything was working before hanging up", "asked if I had any other questions", "confirmed the fix before ending the call", "didn't hang up until I was satisfied", "stayed until I was happy", or similar language). Call closure quality signal — distinct from Rep Followed Through on Promises (post-call follow-through) by occurring at the end of the interaction itself.
Notes: New sub-topic. Call closure quality signal — coachable behavior for ensuring complete resolution before ending interaction.
Taxonomy: Rep Verified Resolution Before Closing->Rep confirmed the issue was fully resolved and checked in with the customer before ending the interaction. Include: 'made sure everything was working before hanging up' 'asked if I had any other questions' 'confirmed the fix before ending the call' 'didn't rush off the phone' 'made sure I was satisfied before closing' 'verified everything was good' 'checked that my issue was resolved' 'stayed until I was happy'. Call closure quality signal — distinct from Rep Followed Through on Promises (post-call follow-through) by occurring at the end of the interaction itself.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer describes the representative taking ACTION or achieving an OUTCOME using specific action/outcome words (including but not limited to: resolved, fixed, helped, handled, took care of, assisted, accommodated, made adjustment, went above and beyond, or similar action descriptors). GENERIC PRAISE ALONE (e.g., "great service," "excellent job") does NOT qualify unless accompanied by action/outcome evidence. IF PROBLEM WAS RESOLVED → classify as Rep Was Helpful (resolving problems is inherently helpful). "Answered my questions" defaults to Rep Was Helpful unless expertise is emphasized. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: ayudó, resolvió, arregló, or similar Spanish action words) also qualify.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Helpful->Customer describes the representative taking ACTION or achieving an OUTCOME using specific action/outcome words (including but not limited to: resolved, fixed, helped, handled, took care of, assisted, accommodated, made adjustment, went above and beyond, or similar action descriptors). GENERIC PRAISE ALONE (e.g., "great service," "excellent job") does NOT qualify unless accompanied by action/outcome evidence. IF PROBLEM WAS RESOLVED → classify as Rep Was Helpful (resolving problems is inherently helpful). "Answered my questions" defaults to Rep Was Helpful unless expertise is emphasized. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: ayudó, resolvió, arregló, or similar Spanish action words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer describes the representative's LACK OF ACTION or FAILURE TO ASSIST using specific inaction words (including but not limited to: refused, wouldn't help, didn't help, useless, no help, or similar inaction descriptors). GENERIC NEGATIVE WORDS ALONE (e.g., "horrible service") do NOT qualify. IF PROBLEM NOT RESOLVED -> DO NOT auto-classify as Rep Not Helpful. Company policies, system limitations, or field tech requirements may prevent resolution despite rep's efforts. Classify Rep Not Helpful ONLY when verbatim indicates rep's ACTIONS, EFFORT, or WILLINGNESS were lacking. "Didn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Helpful. "Couldn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Knowledgeable (ability gap). EXCLUDE: Perceived Trust mentions (dishonesty -> unhelpfulness). Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: no ayudo, inutil, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: High-impact sub-topic with strong share of verbatims. Keep as a standalone sub-topic.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Not Helpful->Customer describes the representative's LACK OF ACTION or FAILURE TO ASSIST using specific inaction words (including but not limited to: refused, wouldn't help, didn't help, couldn't assist, useless, no help, wasted time, or similar inaction descriptors). GENERIC NEGATIVE WORDS ALONE (e.g., "horrible service") do NOT qualify. IF PROBLEM NOT RESOLVED → DO NOT auto-classify as Rep Not Helpful. Company policies, system limitations, or field tech requirements may prevent resolution despite rep's efforts. Classify Rep Not Helpful ONLY when verbatim indicates rep's ACTIONS, EFFORT, or WILLINGNESS were lacking. "Didn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Helpful. "Couldn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Knowledgeable (ability gap). EXCLUDE: Perceived Trust mentions (dishonesty → unhelpfulness). Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: no ayudó, no resolvió, inútil, or similar Spanish inaction words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer describes the rep as slow, inefficient, or unable to work through the issue without excessive fumbling or delays attributable to the rep — not wait time or hold time (including but not limited to: "took forever", "so slow", "kept fumbling around", "didn", "stumbled through everything", "took way too long to do anything", "inefficient", or similar language). EXCLUDE: Long hold time or long wait for rep (process delays, not rep speed). EXCLUDE: Rep Was Impatient / Rushed (rep making the CUSTOMER feel hurried — opposite signal). Distinct from Rep Lacked Knowledge (inability to know) — this is slowness or inefficiency in execution.
Notes: New sub-topic. Negative counterpart to Rep Resolved Issue Quickly / Efficiently. Rep execution speed signal — distinct from process wait time.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Slow / Inefficient->Customer describes the rep as slow, inefficient, or unable to work through the issue without excessive fumbling or delays attributable to the rep — not wait time or hold time. Include: 'took forever' 'so slow' 'kept fumbling around' 'didn't seem to know what they were doing' 'wasted my time' 'couldn't figure it out' 'stumbled through everything' 'took way too long to do anything' 'inefficient'. EXCLUDE: Long hold time or long wait for rep (process delays, not rep speed). EXCLUDE: Rep Was Impatient / Rushed (rep making the CUSTOMER feel hurried — opposite signal). Distinct from Rep Lacked Knowledge (inability to know) — this is slowness or inefficiency in execution.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Rep gave thorough explanation was detail-oriented or reviewed everything completely (including but not limited to: "very thorough", "explained everything", "went through everything", "detailed", "thorough walkthrough", "covered all my questions", "left no stone unturned", "made sure I understood", or similar language). Combine with knowledgeable for training signal.
Notes: Moved from Rep Courtesy to Rep Helpfulness — thoroughness is a service completeness behavior, not a demeanor signal.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Thorough / Detailed->Rep gave thorough explanation was detail-oriented or reviewed everything completely. Include: 'very thorough' 'explained everything' 'went through everything' 'detailed' 'thorough walkthrough' 'covered all my questions' 'left no stone unturned' 'made sure I understood'. Combine with knowledgeable for training signal.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: The rep's actual ability to help resolve or address the customer's need — taking action rather than just being pleasant.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer indicates the rep exceeded expectations or did more than required (including but not limited to: "went above and beyond", "went the extra mile", "did more than expected", "exceeded expectations", "bent over backwards", "went out of their way", "extraordinary effort", "really went above and beyond for me", or similar language). Differentiate from standard helpfulness by the language of exceeding norms.
Notes: High-impact sub-topic with strong share of verbatims. Keep as a standalone sub-topic.
Taxonomy: Rep Went Above and Beyond->Customer indicates the rep exceeded expectations or did more than required. Include: 'went above and beyond' 'went the extra mile' 'did more than expected' 'exceeded expectations' 'bent over backwards' 'went out of their way' 'extraordinary effort' 'really went above and beyond for me'. Differentiate from standard helpfulness by the language of exceeding norms.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: How well the rep understood the product gave accurate information and demonstrated expertise.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Rep transferred knowledge to the customer so they understand their services or can self-serve in the future (including but not limited to: "showed me how to do it myself", "explained why it was happening", "taught me how to use it", "walked me through how it works", "now I understand how to", "helped me understand my bill", "explained what to do next time", "made sure I knew how to handle it going forward", or similar language). Distinct from Rep Was Knowledgeable (rep has knowledge) — this captures knowledge transfer to the customer. Onboarding and self-service quality signal.
Notes: New sub-topic. Knowledge transfer signal — coachable behavior for reducing repeat contacts and improving customer self-sufficiency.
Taxonomy: Rep Educated / Taught the Customer->Rep transferred knowledge to the customer so they understand their services or can self-serve in the future. Include: 'showed me how to do it myself' 'explained why it was happening' 'taught me how to use it' 'walked me through how it works' 'now I understand how to' 'helped me understand my bill' 'explained what to do next time' 'made sure I knew how to handle it going forward'. Distinct from Rep Was Knowledgeable (rep has knowledge) — this captures knowledge transfer to the customer. Onboarding and self-service quality signal.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: How well the rep understood the product gave accurate information and demonstrated expertise.
Sub_Topic: Rep Made an Account or Setup Error
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer attributes a specific account, setup, or service action error directly to something the representative did or changed incorrectly (including but not limited to: "set up the wrong plan," "switched the wrong line," "made a mistake on my account," "applied the wrong discount," "wrong changes were made," "rep made an error," or similar rep-attributed execution error language). EXCLUDE: (1) Systemic billing errors where no specific rep action is cited -> Billing Error / Incorrect Charge (Pricing); (2) IHE unauthorized service additions -> IHE Added Services Without Clear Consent; (3) Account changes made without customer permission -> Unauthorized Account Changes (Process). Distinct from Rep Not Knowledgeable -- requires a concrete wrong action, not just bad advice. CO-CLASSIFY: Billing discrepancy resulted -> also fire Billing Error / Incorrect Charge (Pricing). Error left issue unresolved -> also fire Issue Not Resolved / Still Unresolved (Process).
Notes: New sub-topic. Captures rep-attributed execution errors — wrong account actions, incorrect plan setup, unauthorized changes. Distinct from informational accuracy (Rep Lacked Knowledge) and systemic billing errors (Process).
Taxonomy: Rep Made an Account or Setup Error->Customer attributes a specific account, setup, or service action error directly to something the representative did or changed. Include: 'set up the wrong plan' 'switched the wrong line' 'changed something on my account' 'made a mistake on my account' 'messed up my account' 'applied the wrong discount' 'wrong changes were made' 'changed something without asking me' 'account wasn't set up correctly after my call' 'rep made an error' 'they did something wrong to my account'. EXCLUDE: Systemic billing errors where no specific rep action is cited — those belong in Process (Billing / Charge Error). EXCLUDE: IHE-specific unauthorized additions where the issue is consent, not execution error (see IHE Added Services Without Clear Consent). This is a rep execution error — the rep took an incorrect or unintended account action, regardless of whether they understood the right answer. Distinct from Rep Lacked Knowledge / Gave Wrong Information (informational error) and Rep Not Knowledgeable (competence gap) — this sub-topic requires the customer to describe a concrete wrong action taken on their account.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: How well the rep understood the product gave accurate information and demonstrated expertise.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer explicitly describes the representative's LACK of competence or understanding using SPECIFIC KNOWLEDGE-GAP WORDS (including but not limited to: clueless, incompetent, confused, didn't know, needs training, clearly not trained, doesn't know what they're doing, sloppy, or similar incompetence descriptors). Does NOT apply to: (1) Language barrier or accent issues (see Language Barrier or Comprehension Issue); (2) Error correction patterns ("wrong answer" referring to survey response); (3) Rep providing incorrect information the customer acted on (see Wrong Information). "Couldn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Knowledgeable (ability gap). "Didn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Helpful (effort gap). "Could not explain the bill" = Rep Not Knowledgeable (inability = knowledge gap). Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: no sabia, incompetente, confundido, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: New sub-topic. Definition sourced from revised classification prompt. High co-occurrence expected with Rep Lacked Knowledge / Gave Wrong Information.
Taxonomy: Rep Not Knowledgeable->Customer explicitly describes the representative's LACK of competence or understanding using SPECIFIC KNOWLEDGE-GAP WORDS (including but not limited to: clueless, incompetent, confused, didn't know, gave wrong information, needs training, incapable, or similar incompetence descriptors). EXCLUDE: Language Barrier/accent issues (communication issue, not knowledge — e.g., "hard to understand," "thick accent," "couldn't understand him"). EXCLUDE: Error Correction patterns ("wrong answer" referring to survey response). "Couldn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Knowledgeable (ability gap). "Didn't answer my questions" = Rep Not Helpful (effort gap). "Could not explain the bill" = Rep Not Knowledgeable (inability = knowledge gap). Perceived Trust (wrong information) qualifies ONLY if describing incorrect knowledge, not intentional deception. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: no sabía, incompetente, confundido, or similar Spanish incompetence words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: How well the rep understood the product gave accurate information and demonstrated expertise.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer explicitly describes the representative's competence, expertise, or understanding using SPECIFIC KNOWLEDGE WORDS (including but not limited to: knowledgeable, informative, competent, expert, knew what to do, understood the issue, or similar expertise indicators). SURVEY LANGUAGE ("satisfied with ability," "ability to assist") does NOT qualify -- this is survey question echo. "Answered my questions" defaults to Rep Was Helpful -- only apply Rep Was Knowledgeable when the response indicates the rep answered correctly, confidently, or with clear expertise. "Explained things" defaults to Positive Rep Communication -- only apply when explanation shows expertise or strong command of the issue. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: informado, explico bien, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: New sub-topic. Definition sourced from revised classification prompt. High co-occurrence expected with Rep Was Knowledgeable / Accurate.
Taxonomy: Rep Was Knowledgeable->Customer explicitly describes the representative's competence, expertise, or understanding using SPECIFIC KNOWLEDGE WORDS (including but not limited to: knowledgeable, informative, competent, capable, expert, explained clearly, knew what to do, understood the issue, or similar expertise indicators). SURVEY LANGUAGE ("satisfied with ability," "ability to assist") does NOT qualify — this is survey question echo, not knowledge feedback. "Answered my questions" defaults to Rep Was Helpful; use Rep Was Knowledgeable ONLY when comment indicates rep answered correctly, confidently, or with clear expertise. "Explained things" defaults to Positive Rep Communication; use Rep Was Knowledgeable ONLY when explanation shows expertise, accurate understanding, or strong command of issue. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: informado, capaz, experto, explicó bien, or similar Spanish knowledge words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.

Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer recommends rep for raise, promotion, or special recognition (including but not limited to: "give her a raise", "give him a raise", "give him a promotion", "keep her", "he's a keeper", "she deserves recognition", "employee of the month", or similar language). High satisfaction signal — retention opportunity.

Taxonomy: Give Rep a Raise / Promotion->Customer recommends rep for raise promotion or special recognition. Include: 'give her a raise' 'give him a raise' 'give him a promotion' 'keep her' 'he's a keeper' 'she's a keeper' 'she's a superstar' 'she deserves a raise' 'promote her' 'best employee you have' 'employee of the month'. High satisfaction signal — retention opportunity.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: Customer expresses desire to formally recognize or retain a specific rep.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer names a specific rep negatively — complaint directed at an individual (including but not limited to: "[Name] was terrible", "[Name] was rude", "[Name] didn't help me at all", "[Name] needs training", or similar language). Use for individual coaching identification. Handle with HR sensitivity.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed. High contextual overlap with 'Rep Named Specifically (Positive Mention)' (definition similarity 0.82); consider combining or tightening boundaries.
Taxonomy: Rep Named Specifically (Negative Mention)->Customer names a specific rep negatively — complaint directed at an individual. Include: '[Name] was terrible' '[Name] was rude' '[Name] didn't help at all' 'fire [Name]' '[Name] needs training'. Use for individual coaching identification. Handle with HR sensitivity.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.
Topic_Definition: Customer expresses desire to formally recognize or retain a specific rep.

Notes: Low volume but present. Retain for monitoring; consider roll-up if this remains low across multiple months. High contextual overlap with 'Rep Named Specifically (Negative Mention)' (definition similarity 0.82); consider combining or tightening boundaries.
Taxonomy: Rep Named Specifically (Positive Mention)->Customer names a specific rep positively without raise/promotion language. Include: 'John was great' 'Maria was excellent' 'Sam was helpful' '[Name] did a great job' '[Name] is amazing'. Use for individual rep performance tracking. Extract name for supervisor recognition.

Category_Definition: Feedback about staff performance behavior and interpersonal interactions with reps agents associates or in-home experts across all service channels.

Category_Definition: Feedback about cost pricing fees billing charges or the perceived value of AT&T products and services.
Topic_Definition: How customers feel about the overall level of their bill — whether it is too high acceptable or a good value.
Sub_Topic: Bill Is Too High / Too Expensive
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer expresses that their bill is TOO EXPENSIVE as an ABSOLUTE JUDGMENT using affordability language (including but not limited to: 'too expensive,' 'too high,' 'too much,' 'paying too much,' 'can't afford,' 'outrageous,' 'ridiculous price,' 'overpriced,' 'costs too much,' or similar affordability language). NO comparison to a prior period is required — applies to overall price dissatisfaction. DISTINCTION: 'Overcharged' = Bill Dispute (customer claims a billing error or incorrect charge); 'Overpriced' = Bill Too High (customer finds the price too expensive). Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: muy caro, demasiado, no puedo pagar, or similar Spanish affordability words) also qualify.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed. Definition updated from revised classification prompt. Key addition: Overcharged vs. Overpriced routing distinction.

NO comparison to a prior period is required — applies to overall price dissatisfaction. DISTINCTION: 'Overcharged' = Bill Dispute (customer claims a billing error or incorrect charge); 'Overpriced' = Bill Too High (customer finds the price too expensive). Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: muy caro, demasiado, no puedo pagar, or similar Spanish affordability words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Feedback about cost pricing fees billing charges or the perceived value of AT&T products and services.
Topic_Definition: Feedback specifically about bills going up or unexpected price increases — month over month or mid-contract.
Sub_Topic: Bill Increased / Price Went Up Unexpectedly
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer explicitly states their bill INCREASED using COMPARATIVE LANGUAGE indicating change from a prior period or expectation (including but not limited to: "went up," "increased," "higher than last month," "keeps going up," "rate increase," or similar comparative language). A comparison to previous billing IS REQUIRED. Implicit change language ("now paying more," "suddenly paying more," "bill shocked me") qualifies when context implies increase. EXCLUDE: Pure affordability language without a change event ("too expensive," "can't afford it," "overpriced") -- those are Bill Too High. EXCLUDE: Increases explicitly attributed to a promotion ending ("since my promo ended," "after the deal ran out," "discount expired") -- those are Promotion Expired / Price Jumped After Promo.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed. Definition updated from revised classification prompt. Key addition: Bill Too High routing exclusion and comparative language requirement.
Taxonomy: Bill Increased / Price Went Up Unexpectedly->Customer explicitly states their bill INCREASED using COMPARATIVE LANGUAGE indicating change from a prior period or expectation (including but not limited to: 'went up,' 'increased,' 'higher than before,' 'higher than last month,' 'more than I was paying,' 'keeps going up,' 'jumped,' 'rate increase,' or similar comparative language). A comparison to previous billing or stated expectation IS REQUIRED. Implicit change language ('now paying $X,' 'suddenly paying more,' 'bill shocked me') qualifies when context implies increase. EXCLUDE: Pure affordability language without a change event ('too expensive,' 'costs too much,' 'can't afford it,' 'overpriced,' 'prices are outrageous') — those are Bill Too High. EXCLUDE: Increases explicitly attributed to a promotion or discount ending ('since my promo ended,' 'after the deal ran out,' 'discount expired,' 'promotional rate ended,' 'price went up when my deal ended') — those are Promotion Expired / Price Jumped After Promo.

Category_Definition: Feedback about cost pricing fees billing charges or the perceived value of AT&T products and services.
Topic_Definition: Customer reports incorrect charges unauthorized charges or billing discrepancies that need correction.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer challenges specific charges or amounts and claims an error, unauthorized charge, or being overcharged -- requesting investigation, correction, or reversal. Must assert WRONGNESS OR ERROR, not just dissatisfaction. "Overcharged" (claiming incorrect billing) belongs here, NOT in Bill Too High. Covers three dispute patterns: (1) BILLING ERRORS -- "billing error," "wrong charge," "incorrect bill," "double charged," "charged twice," "I was overcharged"; (2) UNAUTHORIZED CHARGES -- "unauthorized charge on my bill," "never agreed to that charge," "charge I didn't authorize"; (3) PHANTOM SERVICE CHARGES -- billed for a service or line not on the account -- "charged for something I don't have," "billed for services I didn't receive," "charged for a line I cancelled." Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: cobro incorrecto, cargo no autorizado, me cobran por algo que no tengo, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed. Definition updated from revised classification prompt. Key addition: wrongness assertion gate and Overcharged routing confirmation (belongs here, not Bill Too High).
Taxonomy: Billing Error / Incorrect Charge / Overcharge->Customer challenges specific charges or amounts and claims an error, incorrect billing, unauthorized charge, or being overcharged — requesting investigation, correction, or reversal. Must assert WRONGNESS OR ERROR, not just dissatisfaction. 'Overcharged' (claiming to be charged incorrectly or more than should be) belongs here, NOT in Bill Too High. Covers three dispute patterns: (1) BILLING ERRORS — 'billing error,' 'wrong charge,' 'incorrect bill,' 'double charged,' 'charged twice,' 'I was overcharged'; (2) UNAUTHORIZED CHARGES — 'unauthorized charge on my bill,' 'never agreed to that charge,' 'charge I didn't authorize'; (3) PHANTOM SERVICE CHARGES — customer is billed for a service, device, or feature they do not have, did not order, or already cancelled — 'charged for something I don't have,' 'billed for services I didn't receive,' 'charged for a line I cancelled,' 'paying for a service that's not on my account.' Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: cobro incorrecto, me cobraron de mas, cargo no autorizado, error en mi factura, me estan cobrando por algo que no tengo, me cobran por un servicio que cancele, or similar Spanish billing dispute language) also qualify.

Topic_Definition: Customer reports incorrect charges unauthorized charges or billing discrepancies that need correction.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer requests or expects compensation (e.g., credit, refund, or adjustment) due to an error, overcharge, or service issue. Applies to the request itself, regardless of whether the credit was granted (including but not limited to: "refund my money," "give me a refund," "credit my account," "I want my money back," "where is my credit," or similar request/expectation language). Spanish equivalents apply (quiero un reembolso, me deben un credito). Does NOT apply to: (1) trade-in promotional credits missing -> use Trade-In Credit Not Applied / Less Than Promised; (2) BOGO or promotional credits not applying -> use BOGO / Promotion Confusion or Promotion Not Honored / Discount Not Applied. Revenue protection signal -- track for resolution rate.

Category_Definition: Feedback about operational and service delivery processes — how easy or difficult it was to get service resolve issues navigate systems and complete the customer's goal.
Topic_Definition: Whether the technician arrived on time within the window or was late/no-show for the scheduled appointment.
Sub_Topic: Technician No-Show / Did Not Show Up
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer reports a scheduled technician appointment (install, repair, or service) was a no-show or missed, often with no advance notice or after repeated reschedules. Applies when the technician never arrived, the customer waited without contact, or the appointment was repeatedly rescheduled without resolution (including but not limited to: "never showed up," "no one showed up," "waited all day and no one came," "rescheduled three times," "no call no show," or similar no-show language). Spanish equivalents apply (no llego el tecnico, espere todo el dia y nadie vino). Does NOT apply to technician visits that occurred but were unsatisfactory in outcome. Schedule/dispatch failure -- HIGH SEVERITY.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed.
Taxonomy: Technician No-Show / Did Not Show Up->Customer reports a scheduled technician appointment (install, repair, or service) was a no-show or missed, often with no advance notice or after repeated reschedules. Requires that an appointment was expected. Applies when the technician never arrived, the customer waited without contact, or the appointment was repeatedly rescheduled without resolution (including but not limited to: "never showed up," "he didn't show up," "no one showed up," "waited all day and no one came," "missed appointment completely," "rescheduled three times," "no call no show," or similar no-show/missed-appointment language — these are examples only; any verbatim expressing that a scheduled tech visit did not occur qualifies). Spanish equivalents apply (no llegó el técnico, el técnico no se presentó, esperé todo el día y nadie vino, cita cancelada sin aviso). Does NOT apply to technician visits that occurred but were unsatisfactory in quality or outcome. Schedule/dispatch failure — HIGH SEVERITY.

Include: 'tech was late' 'showed up 2 hours late' 'came after the window' 'was supposed to be here at 10' 'late arrival' 'didn't come on time' 'waited past my window'. Distinct from no-show — tech eventually came.

Spanish equivalents apply (esperé mucho, demasiada espera, tardaron en contestar). Does NOT apply to: (1) hold time AFTER already speaking with a rep (use Long/Multiple Holds), (2) wait time for a scheduled callback (not queue time), or (3) general service delays unrelated to reaching a rep.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed.

Category_Definition: Feedback about operational and service delivery processes - how easy or difficult it was to get service resolve issues navigate systems and complete the customer's goal.
Topic_Definition: Timeliness of service interactions including queue wait holds and total interaction duration.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer complains about hold time AFTER already speaking with a representative. Covers two patterns: (1) Duration-based — a single extended hold during the call ("put me on hold for 30 minutes," "left on hold forever," "forgot about me on hold," "was on hold while she checked"); (2) Frequency-based — placed on hold multiple separate times regardless of duration ("kept putting me on hold," "constantly on hold," "put me on hold 5 times," "on and off hold the whole time," "again and again"). Spanish equivalents apply (en espera, me dejó en espera, me puso en espera varias veces). Does NOT apply to: queue/wait time BEFORE reaching any rep (use Long Wait for Rep), hold music before anyone answers (that is queue time), or automated system wait (that is queue time).
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed.
Taxonomy: Long/Multiple Holds->Customer complains about hold time AFTER already speaking with a representative. Covers two patterns: (1) Duration-based — a single extended hold during the call ("put me on hold for 30 minutes," "left on hold forever," "forgot about me on hold," "was on hold while she checked"); (2) Frequency-based — placed on hold multiple separate times regardless of duration ("kept putting me on hold," "constantly on hold," "put me on hold 5 times," "on and off hold the whole time," "again and again"). Spanish equivalents apply (en espera, me dejó en espera, me puso en espera varias veces). Does NOT apply to: queue/wait time BEFORE reaching any rep (use Long Wait for Rep), hold music before anyone answers (that is queue time), or automated system wait (that is queue time).

Category_Definition: Responses that do not contain actionable customer feedback — including blank responses gibberish wrong-customer captures score clarifications opt-out requests and foreign language entries. Used for data hygiene and exclusion from CX analytics.
Topic_Definition: Customer left the verbatim field blank or entered only non-meaningful characters.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Verbatim contains NO SUBSTANTIVE FEEDBACK -- the response is non-actionable regardless of what was entered. Covers four patterns: (1) BLANK / NON-SEMANTIC TEXT -- punctuation-only or symbols with no linguistic content ("." "-" "..." "x"); (2) EXPLICIT OPT-OUTS -- customer consciously declines feedback ("no," "none," "n/a," "nothing," "no comment," "thank you," "ok"); (3) SINGLE WORD SENTIMENT -- sentiment with no topical signal ("good," "great," "fine," "bad," "whatever"); (4) CONVERSATIONAL FILLER -- polite closings with no feedback content ("have a good day," "no issues," "all is well"). This theme should NOT be used as a DEFAULT -- verbatims with ANY substantive content should NOT be tagged as No Feedback; leave unclassified instead. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: nada, todo bien, gracias, or similar) also qualify.
Notes: New sub-topic. Definition sourced from revised classification prompt. Captures explicit opt-outs and conversational declines. Distinct from No Comment (non-semantic text) and Single Word Non-Actionable (sentiment words).
Taxonomy: No Feedback->Verbatim contains NO SUBSTANTIVE FEEDBACK — the response is non-actionable regardless of what was entered. Covers four non-substantive patterns: (1) BLANK / NON-SEMANTIC TEXT — punctuation-only, numbers-only, or symbols that carry no linguistic content ('.' '-' '...' 'x' '5' '10'); (2) EXPLICIT OPT-OUTS — brief responses where customer consciously declines to provide feedback ('no,' 'none,' 'n/a,' 'nothing,' 'no comment,' 'nothing further,' 'I'm good,' 'nope,' 'all good,' 'that's it,' 'thank you,' 'thanks,' 'ok,' 'okay,' or similar); (3) SINGLE WORD SENTIMENT — a sentiment word with no topical signal ('good,' 'great,' 'fine,' 'bad,' 'terrible,' 'yes,' 'cool,' 'meh,' 'whatever,' or similar); (4) CONVERSATIONAL FILLER — short phrases that are polite closings or acknowledgments with no feedback content ('have a good day,' 'keep up the good work,' 'no issues,' 'all is well'). Typically 5 words or fewer across all patterns. This theme should NOT be used as a DEFAULT when other themes do not apply — verbatims containing ANY substantive content (even if off-topic or unclassifiable) should NOT be tagged as No Feedback; leave unclassified instead. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: nada, ninguno, todo bien, gracias, sin comentarios, or similar Spanish non-substantive responses) also qualify.

Does NOT apply to: Service cancellation requests ("I want to cancel," "cancelling my account," "leaving AT&T" —these are churn signals, not survey opt-outs. "Stop" used in complaint context ("stop overcharging me") does NOT qualify. Spanish equivalents (including but not limited to: alto, parar, cancelar suscripcion, or similar Spanish opt-out words) also qualify.

Category_Definition: Responses that do not contain actionable customer feedback — including blank responses gibberish wrong-customer captures score clarifications opt-out requests and foreign language entries. Used for data hygiene and exclusion from CX analytics.
Topic_Definition: Customer comments about the survey itself — its length, design, timing, or frequency — rather than providing feedback about AT&T service.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Customer comments about the survey experience rather than providing AT&T service feedback. Covers two complaint types: (1) SURVEY DESIGN / LENGTH -- "this survey is too long," "too many questions," "survey doesn't apply," "survey is confusing," "fix your survey"; (2) SURVEY FREQUENCY -- "too many surveys," "keep sending me surveys," "I already did this survey," "stop asking me for feedback," "why am I getting another survey." CO-CLASSIFY: Verbatims containing BOTH an opt-out request AND a frequency or design complaint (e.g., "Stop, I am receiving too many surveys") should fire both this sub-topic AND Stop Survey / Opt-Out Request. EXCLUDE: Pure opt-out requests with no frequency or design complaint context -- those are Stop Survey / Opt-Out Request only.
Notes: Material signal with usable volume. Keep as standalone and tune examples as needed.
Taxonomy: Survey Feedback->Customer comments about the survey experience rather than providing AT&T service feedback. Covers two complaint types: (1) SURVEY DESIGN / LENGTH — 'this survey is too long,' 'too many questions,' 'why so many questions,' 'survey doesn't apply,' 'questions don't make sense,' 'survey is confusing,' 'took too long,' 'fix your survey'; (2) SURVEY FREQUENCY — 'too many surveys,' 'keep sending me surveys,' 'I already did this survey,' 'stop asking me for feedback,' 'survey every time I call,' 'I already answered this,' 'why am I getting another survey.' CO-CLASSIFY: Verbatims containing BOTH an opt-out request AND a frequency or design complaint (e.g., 'Stop, I am receiving too many surveys') should fire both this sub-topic AND Stop Survey / Opt-Out Request. EXCLUDE: Pure opt-out requests with no frequency or design complaint context — those are Stop Survey / Opt-Out Request only.

Topic_Definition: Response contains random characters keyboard mashing or text that cannot be interpreted as meaningful feedback.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Response is gibberish random characters or clearly unintentional input (including but not limited to: "asdfgh", "jjjjjj", "qwerty", "123456", "hhhhhh", "test test", or similar language). random emoji strings accidental dictation noise. Exclude from all analysis.
Notes: Very low volume signal. Consider aggregating with nearest sibling unless a distinct operational owner requires separate tracking.
Taxonomy: Random Characters / Keyboard Input->Response is gibberish random characters or clearly unintentional input. Include: 'asdfgh' 'jjjjjj' 'qwerty' '123456' 'hhhhhh' 'test test' random emoji strings accidental dictation noise. Exclude from all analysis.

Category_Definition: Responses that do not contain actionable customer feedback — including blank responses gibberish wrong-customer captures score clarifications opt-out requests and foreign language entries. Used for data hygiene and exclusion from CX analytics.
Topic_Definition: Survey was sent to wrong person or customer is commenting on an interaction they did not have.
Sub_Topic_Definition: Verbatim states the survey was received in error because the respondent did not interact with the company. This applies to identity mismatch, not input errors (including but not limited to: "I didn't call AT&T," "never spoke with anyone," "this survey was sent in error," "I'm not an AT&T customer," "you have the wrong person," "this phone number doesn't belong to me," "someone else used my account," "I never visited the store," or similar identity-mismatch language). Spanish equivalents apply (no soy cliente, no llamó, no habló con nadie, alguien más usó mi cuenta, nunca fui a la tienda). Does NOT apply to: Score Error Correction (wrong survey rating, hit wrong number, meant to put 5 not 1) — that is input correction, NOT identity mismatch.
Notes: High-impact sub-topic with strong share of verbatims. Keep as a standalone sub-topic.
